data.title: Broadcom: AI Infrastructure Leader With Long Growth Runway
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) is a technology company that focuses on semiconductor solutions and infrastructure software. This is a roughly $1.6 trillion market capitalization stock. Although the stock rallied by nearly a triple-digit percentage, I argue the company has more upside to go. AVGO remains an artificial intelligence infrastructure leader with significant tailwinds, supporting a bullish outlook.

AVGO trades at a forward P/E of approximately 37 versus the FY2026 EPS estimate, which signals a 50% premium versus the sector median of 24x earnings multiple. Although this may suggest that upside appears limited, Broadcom delivered 28% revenue growth over the past 12 months, indicating 3.4x outperformance versus peers at 8.17%. I argue that Broadcom may actually be cheap if growth delivers.

The semiconductor leader operates in the cyclical industry and is tied to execution risk related to large-scale delivery and custom design solutions. Although this may appear concerning, I think tailwinds outweigh the headwinds, and Broadcom remains the market-leading company that is well-positioned to drive the AI-related wave, fueling the company's bullish outlook.

I rate Broadcom a Strong Buy with a $418 price target over the next 12 months. This presents approximately a 23% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. I believe the stock price may mirror bottom-line growth, as I think AVGO can successfully translate AI-driven demand into high double-digit bottom-line expansion. Time will tell, but I think Broadcom may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking a dominant name with a significant moat.

FQ3 2025: A High-Double-Digit Top And Bottom Line Growth Fueling A Bullish Narrative

On September 4, the company delivered FQ3 2025 results. This has been a strong quarterly performance for the company, underscoring a high double-digit top and bottom-line growth and solid momentum in demand. Investors rewarded the company with a roughly 9% stock price appreciation over the following trading day.

Broadcom posted $15.95 billion in revenue, implying a 22% increase on a year-over-year basis, and $129 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $1.69 in diluted EPS, approximately a 36% surge versus $1.24 in diluted EPS in the same quarter last year, and $0.03 above analysts' estimates. These results appear to be phenomenal and support a bullish continuation.

In the press release, management highlighted that revenue growth was mainly driven by AI accelerators, the networking business, and VMware. It was also underscored that AI-related revenue has skyrocketed 63% on a year-over-year basis, reaching $5.2 billion. I think this is impressive, indicates a significant market-leading position, and argues for continuation if AI demand continues.

The company has been running on all cylinders. The semiconductor solutions delivered $9.16 billion in revenue, about 26% growth on a year-over-year basis, accounting for 57% of total top-line contribution. The infrastructure software posted $6.79 billion in revenue, representing a 17% increase over the past 12 months, and 43% of total revenue. This is a significant performance, arguing for well-diversified business operations and meaningful demand across both categories. This justifies AVGO trading at a premium multiple.

Broadcom also delivered $10.7 billion in adjusted EBITDA, representing approximately 67% of total revenue, and more than $7 billion in free cash flow, which accounted for 44% of total revenue, and implied a 47% increase on a year-over-year basis. This supports my thesis of the company being highly profitable.

To conclude, AVGO delivered an outstanding quarter, indicating premium business, arguing for a bullish continuation. The company's fundamentals are improving, Broadcom maintains a market-leading stance, and remains well-positioned to benefit from AI-driven demand.

AVGO is anticipated to deliver FQ4 and FY2025 results on December 11, after the market closes. Wall Street analysts are expecting $17.46 billion in revenue, indicating 24% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $1.87 in diluted EPS, highlighting approximately 32% expansion versus the same quarter last year. For FY2025, the top-line is expected to arrive at $63.34 billion, nearly 23% growth versus FY2024, and $6.75 in diluted EPS, representing a 39% surge versus last year.

I think the company is well-positioned to achieve these targets. The technology leader is experiencing solid momentum in demand, and double-digit growth argues for high-quality services. There are obvious reasons why AVGO is a $1.6 trillion stock; one could argue, an appealing fit to the group of Magnificent 7, if ever considered for an addition. If positive sentiment tied to AI demand is maintained, and capex spending is not anticipated to slow down, Broadcom is likely to successfully ride the AI wave further.

Significant Top And Bottom Line Growth Justifies The Premium Multiple

Broadcom is trading at a forward P/E of roughly 37 versus FY2026, and that appears to be about a 55% premium versus the average company in the S&P500 at 23x earnings multiple. Although this appears to be elevated, I argue AVGO may actually be cheap if growth delivers, and AI-driven demand continues.

The chart above suggests that Broadcom is comfortable trading at elevated levels, and there remains room for multiple expansions if premium business performance is maintained. On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 1.71, the company appears to be roughly 2% overvalued versus peers, which remains an insignificant premium, arguing for a bullish continuation.

On the downside, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 26.38 and 25.53, respectively, reveal that Broadcom trades at a substantial premium versus peers, highlighting a 7x higher valuation at the midpoint compared to the sector medians of 3.44 and 3.38. Although I think AVGO deserves a significantly higher premium than peers, this may cap upside in the near term, especially if top-line performance comes in weaker-than-anticipated.

Furthermore, Broadcom is a medium-leveraged business, and that may also be concerning for long-term investors. The company has $64.23 billion in total obligations and $10.72 billion in cash. This translates into a meaningful financial burden, implying $3.365 billion paid in interest expenses to service its debt on a TTM basis.

On the upside, AVGO remains an outstanding top- and bottom-line growth story, arguing for a bullish setup. Over the past 12 months, the company achieved 28% revenue growth, representing more than 3x outperformance versus peers at 8%. The bottom line surged by 228% over the past year, a significant outperformance versus peers at 14%. If the company can sustain such top- and bottom-line growth at a high double-digit pace, the current 50% premium appears insignificant. This would also argue for the continuation of the upward momentum in the stock price.

The company's other strength remains phenomenal operating margins. Broadcom operates at a gross profit margin (TTM) of 77%, which highlights a 55% premium versus peers at 50%. The net income margin (TTM) of nearly 32% highlights a massive advantage versus the sector median of 5%. This supports my bullish thesis and indicates AVGO is a highly profitable business.

To conclude, the company is a market-leading business that grows its top and bottom line at a more rapid pace than its peers. Based on the analysis, Broadcom trades at a moderate premium, but there is room for multiple expansions. Although the company remains moderately leveraged, it operates at outstanding margins, which argue for a positive outlook. Broadcom remains a significant beneficiary of AI-driven demand, and I think the trend may continue further.

As highlighted above, Wall Street analysts expect $9.31 in diluted EPS for FY2026, and that puts the company at the forward P/E of 37. Versus the FY2025 projection, AVGO trades at a 51x earnings multiple, which supports my thesis that forward-looking markets may price in robust performance in advance.

Therefore, if we apply a 45x earnings multiple, which would indicate a 15% discount to the FY2025 estimate, but approximately a 25% premium versus FY2026, to $9.31 in diluted EPS expectations for the next year, we arrive at my price target of $418. The sentiment around the company is positive, and if management can continue to deliver such an appealing performance, I think the trend may proceed further.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Broadcom that could meaningfully impact the stock price performance in the short term.

The volatility seems to have returned to the broader markets, and we haven't seen this since April this year. If the sentiment weakens, the broader stock market may take a breather, which could result in a sell-off. If that happens, Broadcom is likely to correct along with other names, highlighting more than a triple-digit stock price return over the past 12 months and the potential of profit-taking happening.

The technology company is also heavily dependent on AI-driven demand. If IT spending slows down, the sentiment may weaken, and the market may not be willing to pay a premium for the stock. So far, the companies, such as Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon, have increased their capex spending, and if the trend continues, such companies as Broadcom will remain key beneficiaries.

Although I believe Broadcom deserves a premium valuation, if top- and bottom-line performance weakens, the stock could correct. The forward-looking markets may also suppress valuations until headwinds fade, and that could result in short-term underperformance versus broader markets.

AVGO faces customer concentration risk. For instance, Apple accounts for a high portion of the company's wireless revenue, and in FQ2 2024, this was roughly 16% of AVGO's quarterly top-line result. Although AAPL remains a premium client, this may be concerning if the technology giant finds an alternative provider in the space.

On the other hand, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, Broadcom appears to be well-positioned to continue riding the AI-powered wave.

Conclusion: Broadcom Is A Strong Buy

I think Broadcom may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who see Mag-7-type quality and exposure to high-demand, strong moat technology names. The company operates at outstanding margins, grows its top and bottom line at a rapid pace, and is tied to AI-powered tailwinds, which may continue further.

AVGO remains moderately leveraged, and that could cloud the positive outlook. The stock trades at a premium versus both peers and the industry, and although I think Broadcom deserves an elevated valuation, if sentiment shifts, the stock may correct. However, I argue that tailwinds outweigh the headwinds, and that the technology leader may actually be cheap if growth delivers.

That's why I rate Broadcom a Strong Buy with a $418 price target over the next year, suggesting a 23% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. 46 Wall Street analysts project a $395 price target, presenting a 15% upside potential. I feel more bullish, but it will be interesting to monitor whether tailwinds materialize.

data.title: Retirement: 2 Healthcare Giants To Buy At Discounted Prices
data.description:
Co-authored with Kody's Dividends

For the vast majority of us, we don't like to interact with the healthcare sector.

As we get older, we often have to see more doctors, more physicians, and take more medications to maintain our health. It's been said that when we're younger, we trade our health for wealth, and when we're older, we trade our wealth for health.

For many dividend investors, the healthcare sector is one that they often will avoid, likely because there are many different options and they potentially clash with one another because of their products being in competition.

Today, I want to look at two wonderful healthcare sector opportunities. These are investment opportunities that are trading at very attractive values, have long histories of dividend growth, and are primed to give you excellent total returns.

Let's dive in!

Pick No. 1 - Merck (MRK): Powering Dividend Growth Beyond Keytruda

The first pick is the pharmaceutical giant, Merck. At a macro level, Kody's investment thesis from this May listicle is intact. Statista forecasts that global pharmaceutical spending will rise by almost 5% each year from around $1.2 trillion in 2025 to almost $1.5 trillion by 2029.

On a more company-specific level, we remain bullish on Merck for a few key reasons.

While the company is most known for the best-selling drug in the world, Keytruda ($8.14 billion, or about 47% of its Q3 2025 sales), it's not a one-trick pony. As of Q3 2025, the company had a handful of other drugs that were putting up blockbuster numbers (e.g., $1 billion or more in annualized sales) themselves. Nine others, to be more precise.

These include the Gardasil human papillomavirus vaccine franchise, the Januvia/Janumet type 2 diabetes franchises, and the Lynparza oncology franchises.

Merck also remains upbeat about its post-Keytruda future. On the one hand, the company is working to limit the sales drop-off of Keytruda. Merck's more convenient subcutaneous administration method (versus the usual IV route) was approved by the FDA in September. That saves time for both patients and healthcare providers. When Keytruda eventually faces biosimilar competition, this could allow the company to retain more patients.

On its Q3 2025 Earnings Call, Chairman and CEO Robert Davis also reiterated the company's expectations for over $50 billion of commercial opportunities by the mid-2030s.

The Winrevair pulmonary arterial hypertension franchise still figures to be instrumental in this future. Sales rocketed 141% year-over-year to $360 million in Q3 2025. Following news of an updated FDA label, analysts at Citi have pegged the drug's annual peak sales potential at upwards of $6 billion.

Merck's pipeline remains deep as well, with more than 50 programs in phase 2 clinical trials and over 30 in phase 3 clinical trials. The company continues to strengthen its portfolio, too.

The latest move to do so came with the completion of the $10 billion deal for Verona Pharma last month. With this transaction, Merck assumes ownership of Ohtuvayre, the fast-growing COPD medicine. Considering that the medicine is on pace for the strongest COPD launch in history, Jefferies analyst Andrew Tsai anticipates that annual peak sales could reach $4 billion.

What's more, Merck has the finances to strategically invest in other deals down the road. That's backed up by its A+ S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

Merck's 3.9% dividend yield also remains well above the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.2% yield, which makes it an interest income pick. This is especially the case because its payout ratio should be around 36% in 2025. That gives it room to extend its 14-year dividend growth streak.

Merck looks to be an appealing investment opportunity. However, there are still risks that could challenge the investment thesis. These include regulatory risks and a potential inability to navigate Keytruda's upcoming loss of exclusivity.

Merck's regulatory risks include the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and President Trump's Most Favored Nation Executive Order.

In the case of the former, any recently launched drugs that become blockbusters could find themselves subject to the IRA in the future. Such negotiations with Medicare could weigh on prices and profit margins for impacted drugs.

Talk of the latter risk occasionally pops up, but we don't view that as much of an imminent risk. This is because President Trump signed a similar executive order in 2020, which was eventually blocked by courts. A BioSpace article from earlier this year outlines how these legal concerns remain unresolved and how the system isn't set up for such a policy to be viable if it is legally upheld.

Finally, the other risk to Merck is that its efforts in recent years may not be enough to offset Keytruda's patent cliff. If its product portfolio, internal pipeline, and acquisitions don't live up to expectations, that could set back the investment thesis.

Pick No. 2 - Elevance Health (ELV): A Top-Notch Dividend Grower On Sale

Elevance Health is facing some headwinds in the near term.

In its Q3 2025 Earnings Call, President and CEO Gail Boudreaux indicated that Medicaid margins are going to drop by at least 125 basis points in 2026. This is because of a combination of membership reverifications, state program changes, and higher utilization trends. That explains why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for Elevance Health's adjusted diluted EPS to decrease by another 9.5% in 2026 to $27.14 (on top of a 9.2% decline in 2025 to $30).

Overall, we think that these negatives are still outweighed by Elevance Health's long-term positives. The company serves 109 million consumers across its medical, pharmacy, behavioral, clinical, and home health solutions. That footprint is among the largest in the country.

Long-term demand drivers are likely to hold up as well. Rising prescription volumes and a more specialized drug mix are key pluses for Elevance Health. The essential nature of the company's services also affords it pricing power.

That's why the company expects long-term revenue growth to be in the high-single-digit range annually. This varies from mid- to high-single-digits in Health Benefits to as much as a high teens to low 20s CAGR for the Carelon Services business.

On the bottom line, Elevance Health is also focusing on becoming a more efficient enterprise. By the end of 2025, the company will provide access to its AI-enabled virtual assistant. As this is rolled out, it's expected to further reduce calls per member, reducing its administrative costs. Moves like these are part of how Elevance Health plans to expand its adjusted operating margin to between 6.5% and 7%.

Add in share repurchases and occasional acquisitions, and this provides a path to return to double-digit annual adjusted diluted EPS growth beyond 2026. Elevance Health's A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook is another important source of strength that can support more M&A activity in the years to come.

Its 2.2% dividend yield enhances the investment case even further. The payout ratio is expected to be in the low-20% range for 2025. That sets Elevance Health to build on its 13-year dividend growth streak.

While we believe Elevance Health will bounce back, there are risks to the investment thesis. These include customer concentration risk and cybersecurity risks.

The fortunes of Elevance Health are inextricably linked to the U.S. government. In 2024, the company generated 31% of its revenue from agencies of the U.S. government, with the majority of these revenues coming from its Health Benefits segment (page 6 of 492 of Elevance Health's 10-K Filing). This is why it will be important to monitor the relationship between the U.S. government and Elevance Health moving forward. Any unfavorable regulatory reforms or a strained relationship could pressure the company's growth prospects.

Elevance Health's size and scale also open it up to attempted cyber breaches. If any major cyber breaches happen, sensitive stakeholder data could be compromised. That could result in billions of dollars in litigation, as well as regulatory fines. These occurrences could also put a damper on Elevance Health's fundamentals.

Conclusion

Both Marek and Elevance Health are trading at values we find highly attractive. We expect them to provide strong total returns through dividend payments and growth, as well as capital appreciation. Dividend King members enjoy our price targets as part of their membership.

Dividend growth can occur in two ways within your portfolio. The first is the holdings of your portfolio raising their dividends organically. The second way to see dividend growth in your portfolio is mechanical; that is, actively investing or reinvesting capital in your portfolio to cause your overall income to grow.

I like to think of my portfolio as an orchard. I expect every single tree in my orchard to produce fruit that I can enjoy, and to produce more fruit than the year prior through careful maintenance and pruning. I also take some of that fruit and plant it to get another tree in the future. This way, my portfolio's produce, my dividends, grows through both organic methods and mechanical methods. Meanwhile, the capital that I have invested in these opportunities, I expect to continue to grow healthily, just like I would a tree. This is exactly the mindset we have as we build our portfolios together in our community. I hope that you are building your portfolio with that mindset as well! Enjoy capital gains and a great dividend harvest.

Plant your seeds. Watch them grow. Enjoy a bountiful harvest.

data.title: Apple: A Resilient Giant In A Risky Macro World
data.description:
So far in 2025, Apple Inc. (AAPL) has underperformed the broader market.

Though I think it's likely to end up outperforming it by the end of the year, it's not the best pick out of the top mega-cap stocks out there. YTD, AAPL is up 9%, sitting in the middle of the Mag7 pack in terms of total returns.

It still did better than Amazon (AMZN), as tariff risks hit the stock harder, and Meta Platforms (META), due to its own idiosyncratic challenges. But it consistently underperformed half of its Mag7 peers.

But due to certain macro conditions, AAPL remains an excellent defensive option. It definitely isn't a high-growth opportunity given what we know after its latest earnings results or a bargain at this price, but it is well-positioned to keep delivering decent returns amid important macroeconomic risks due to its structural strengths and how the market perceives the business.

Macro Lens

Inflation Expectations

Inflation has been sticky, with CPI and Core CPI hovering around 3% this year. Consumers anticipate higher inflation in the near term.

This is consistent with the expectation that tariffs are going to contribute to rising prices in the short run as businesses exhaust the inventories that they acquired before higher tariffs went into effect.

For Apple, this is a slight headwind in terms of margin resilience because it is likely to absorb the higher input costs and not raise prices in the near term. After higher tariffs went into effect, the company focused on alternative methods to offset part of the cost. However, Apple's services segment contributes approximately one-quarter of revenue and grows at a solid pace, offsetting this pressure to a meaningful extent.

For the stock, these inflation expectations are unlikely to prove punishing because the market often reacts to rapid changes in expectations and rates that could threaten consumer spending in discretionary items and AAPL's multiple. With inflation stable around 3% and an expected slight and slow increase, this is not a major headwind.

Labor Market Conditions

In 2025, the labor market has weakened substantially. Job growth has been decelerating, the unemployment rate has risen, and the number of claims for unemployment insurance has remained high.

Looking forward, unemployment should rise to 4.5% according to Global Investment Research. The headwind for a company like Apple is minor here as well, as this picture implies stretched upgrade timelines, rather than a significant cutback in spending. On the flip side, a softer labor market restricts wage growth, which in turn eases the operating expense pressure for Apple.

Monetary Policy Path

Now, this is where the above conditions imply a tailwind for Apple.

Last time, the Fed lowered the policy rate of interest for a second consecutive meeting this year. Though the Chairman signalled less certainty about this pace of rate cuts, the discussion above points to a faster way to a "neutral" rate than only half a year ago, that the labor market was more solid and inflation was rising again after closing in on the Fed's target of 2%.

Many officials and analysts agree that inflation is sticky and should rise in the short term, but tariffs won't be a long-term problem. On the other hand, few exhibit the same confidence about the labor market. The road might be bumpy and we might not see another rate cut this year, but the path towards a more neutral policy interest rate is clearer than it was just months ago. With a cooler labor market lengthening upgrade cycles, cheaper borrowing offsets that effect to some degree. But more importantly, AAPL's valuation should benefit as a result of its high sensitivity to long-term yields, which are expected to fall in this environment.

Growth Trends

Real GDP growth has already been volatile this year as tariffs have injected uncertainty across consumers and businesses. It contracted in the first quarter and then rebounded in the second, reflecting the inventory buildup and the subsequent decline in imports. In the first half of this year, it averaged a ~1.4% growth. The outlook for economic growth points to a below-average rate.

This is a headwind for equities in general, but it's the single most important one that makes AAPL more attractive to me. Though slower economic growth in the near term may result in slower hardware sales, it makes the business relatively more attractive for the market. Quality mega-caps become more defensive in such a regime and investors rotate out of small caps, cyclicals, and unprofitable tech businesses. A high-margin business (for a hardware one) that is also cash-rich and has a global customer base is a more conservative pick. That doesn't mean AAPL should outperform other mega caps in such a scenario; just that it's more likely to do better on a risk-adjusted basis.

Currency Outlook

YTD, the US dollar has fallen substantially, with most of the action occurring early this year.

Based on the rest of the conditions related to inflation, the labor market, and monetary policy, this trend should persist. RBC Capital Markets also points to the government shutdown and the Fed's dovish stance as drivers of the narrowed rate differentials; its base case is for a weaker dollar in the near term.

Similarly, Morgan Stanley Research forecasts another 10% decline for the greenback by the end of next year due to the pending impact of tariffs on growth and unemployment.

Since Apple is a very currency-sensitive company because more than half of its revenue is generated abroad, if the dollar weakens as it's expected, that implies higher currency-adjusted revenue and, all else being equal, no need to raise prices internationally to protect margins.

Fundamentals

In its Q4 quarterly results, Apple continued to exhibit its mature and growing business with a few red flags shareholders should keep in mind. It generated revenue of $102.5 billion, which is 8% higher than the same period in 2024. Adjusted diluted EPS was $1.85, up 13% on a year-over-year basis.

Services revenue contributed significantly at $28.75 billion, reflecting a ~15% YoY increase. iPhone revenue growth was slower, growing about 6.1% YoY; at $49.03 billion, it missed the Wall Street estimate by a bit more than one billion dollars. In China, revenue was lower at $14.49 billion (from $15.03 billion) on a YoY basis, which is consistent with a softer-growth regime but nevertheless a sticky point for the market as it creates friction in the overall bull case for Apple. This is a large market for Apple and investors are concerned. The soft-growth environment is also reflected in the slight decline of Wearables/Home/Accessories revenue ($9.01 billion in Q4, down 0.3% YoY). That being said, this figure beat consensus estimates by 8.49% as the company launched the Apple Watch Ultra 3 and AirPods Pro 3.

Looking forward, the guidance is promising as management expects about 10-12% growth in revenue in the first quarter of 2026 and this would be the best quarter to date. Similarly, iPhone growth is estimated to grow by double digits in the next quarter, a meaningful acceleration since the last quarter, as we enter the holiday season.

Now, valuation is an important part of assessing Apple's relative attractiveness and though I see it a bit stretched, it's reflective of the macro conditions we talked about above. Although growth is slower, the market does seem to appreciate the stock in such an environment.

Its forward earnings multiple of 33.2x is close to that which MSFT and AMZN are trading at. Google (GOOG) at 26x appears to be more attractive. This tells us that the market currently assigns a premium for stability. With AAPL trading above both MSFT and GOOG, investors seem to be more interested in safety, rather than momentum, right now. Its FCF yield of 2.42%, however, is the highest among those names. Its Price to Sales ratio is also high, only surpassed by that of MSFT.

Technicals

As you can see below, AAPL is still in a healthy, albeit tired, uptrend. On one hand, the price has been close to the upper Bollinger band and there have been no lower-band tests or failed pushes. On the other hand, the candles are becoming shorter and flatter.

Still, momentum is enduring with the RSI at ~64, consistent with recent levels. AAPL also experienced a golden cross back in the summer.

Risks

The elephant in the room is the market in China, which reflects a potential structural issue. This is a big market, as I mentioned above (~15% of revenue), and though Apple is not a company you'd bet against in the long run, the market may be punishing in the short run.

Another risk to be mindful of is the valuation; as AAPL is trading at a premium to some other mega-cap names but isn't growing as aggressively right now, a pullback is likely if the sentiment becomes more optimistic, since the market seems to assign a premium for the stock's defensiveness right now.

A near-term risk is the fact that the market is currently rewarding other companies that are ahead of Apple in terms of generative AI applications. Apple has been trying to paint an AI narrative that suits the business and if that breaks due to low enthusiasm in OS updates or future sales, a pullback is again likely.

Conclusion

All in all, Apple is not a high-growth or a value opportunity. Its attractiveness comes from the relation between the current macroeconomic risks and its structural strengths relative to other large businesses. I will maintain my buy rating and my position in the stock as long as this thesis is intact.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Danaos Q3: Drybulk Isn't Growing, But The Reasons Seem Valid
data.description:
The last time I wrote on Danaos Corp. (DAC) was on April 11, 2025, where I said this:

I think management will keep a heavy foot on the buyback pedal while its stock languishes in the low-single digit valuation multiples of 2.5x for its price-to-forward adjusted EPS and 0.35 for its price-to-forward book. I do see another board authorization coming up as DAC hits the $168.8 million mark on its current $200 million authorization.

Surprise, surprise! Three days later, on April 14, DAC added another $100 million on top of its existing authorization. In the nine month since the Q424 report that the article focused on, DAC's total buybacks are now up to $213.6 million for a total share count of just over 3 million. That averages out to around $71.2 per share, give or take, so that's a significant discount to the current price above $96.

Having said that, I don't think it's a good idea for DAC to remain aggressive on buybacks at current levels. Given how focused the company has always been on preserving cashflows, I can already see a slowdown on that front over the past three reported quarters, with the company reporting about $53.2 million in repurchases in that time. It's nearly 10x what DAC spent in 2024 ($5.7 million) over the same 9M period, but you have to take into account the fact that it was trading close to $100 again by June 2024, so I think it's pretty prudent of management to cadence their buybacks that way.

I see the same thing happening over the next couple of quarters as the stock once again approaches that dreaded resistance level. At this level of buyback yield, though, it would still make sense to keep buying back its shares. It's simply yet another validation of the company's undervaluation that I discussed in my earlier articles. Undervalued at near $100? I still think so, but before we get to valuation, let's look at what the Q3 report had in store.

What Does Q3 Tell Us?

You're probably familiar with the headline numbers already, so let me quickly run through a few key points.

First of all, despite the marginal 1.8% revenue growth rate, significantly higher D&A as well as G&A forced DAC's operating income to come in lower on a YoY basis. The D&A part is good for cashflow because it's a non-cash item that's added back to net income. In fact, net income itself was significantly higher than Q3 of the prior year because of a $9 million gain on investments, as well as the lower equity investment loss (YoY) offsetting debt extinguishment loss.

The result was $130.6 million in net income against $123 million last year. When I said "good for cashflow", I meant to highlight the $11+ million increase in OCF from the year-ago period, and that's a good thing because that brings the 9M OCF in line with that of last year. We see that cashflow preservation mandate firmly in place.

On the balance sheet, DAC looks a little healthier this quarter, and a lot more liquid. Current assets came in at $867 million against the prior-period figure of $671 million, and this is despite zero proceeds from long-term debt against $118 million last year, and a $9.4 million debt paydown against $6.3 million in the year-ago quarter. I can see how management has astutely reduced its new vessel commitments from nearly $240 million last year to just $92 million this quarter. That type of spending cycle is typical with DAC. In addition, the payments on deferred drydocking and survey commitments were nearly halved.

Together, these adjustments increased DAC's cash position by a little over $50 million, hence its stronger balance sheet. On the debt side, DAC issued new unsecured 7Y bonds to the tune of $500 million that also boosted its balance sheet, but what I find appealing is the 6.875% coupon. CEO Coustas called it "one of the most competitively priced deals ever achieved in the shipping industry for an unsecured bond with such tenor".

That's not a small achievement because it's going to help pay off the $300 million 8.500% coupon bonds due in 2028, adding less pressure on the balance sheet and supporting better cashflows into 2026 and beyond.

The Drybulk Story Pauses for Breath

Now, let's get to the meat and potatoes of this discussion - the ongoing efforts to add more drybulk capacity. There wasn't much of a movement in drybulk capacity, but utilization was at 100% through the quarter compared to 85% in the year-ago period. We're finally starting to see some material net income from drybulk, and what this is now doing is boosting overall net income and improving EPS - like a little helper to the core containership segment.

This isn't what I expected, because I liked the fact that drybulk was growing into a new revenue stream. Sure, it's still only about 5% of DAC's overall asset base, but growth is much better here. You can see how much better drybulk TCEs this quarter were ($19,910/day against $16,179/day last year), so I think management missed a chance of generating even more net income on their 100% drybulk fleet utilization. That might just be my short-term reaction flaring up, because I know management is being cautiously optimistic. In the Q2 call, Coustas had said this:

On the Drybulk side, we saw some seasonal firming in the market, but broader weakness persists, largely due to deflationary conditions in China. While we continue to evaluate opportunities in the sector, asset value for modern tonnage remain elevated, and we are in no rush to commit capital in an uncertain macroeconomic environment.

He brought it up again at the Q3 call, where the tone is a little more positive. So, we do have a long-term horizon here but the timing seems inappropriate. While ruling out newbuilds all together, Coustas did underline DAC's interest in looking for good deals on vessels:

we definitely want to increase it. For the time being in the newbuilding front, still these vessels do not make sense. So we're trying to expand selectively in the secondhand market and mainly trying to identify good quality vessels.

The opportunity is definitely there in drybulk, with the past five months or so showing a sharp increase on the BCI or Baltic Capesize Index, which is the most appropriate index for the drybulk segment DAC operates in.

How Does All of This Impact My Thesis for DAC?

On the performance front, I still see prudent cashflow management. I don't see aggressiveness on the drybulk side, but moving too fast might create a conflict with cashflow preservation. At least, that's what management's moves seem to indicate.

I can accept it, but I disagree with this view because voyage expenses for DACs drybulk vessels were significantly lower this quarter compared to last year.

More analytically, voyage expenses of our dry bulk vessels segment decreased by $4.5 million, to $4.7 million in the three months ended September 30, 2025 compared to $9.2 million voyage expenses in the three months ended September 30, 2024. For the three months ended September 30, 2025, voyage expenses of our dry bulk vessels comprised of $1.4 million in commissions and $3.3 million in other voyage expenses, mainly comprised of bunkers cost and port expenses, compared to $1.2 million in commissions and $8.0 million in other voyage expenses for the three months ended September 30, 2024, reflecting an increase in time charter employment of our dry bulk vessels during the three months ended September 30, 2025 compared to the three months ended September 30, 2024.

The main point is the "bunkers cost and port expenses" line item. $3.3 million in Q3-25 against $8 million in Q3-24, and this despite higher utilization. One of the reasons is bunker fuel prices, which have been falling since their peaks near the end of 2023.

Oil prices, in general, are expected to decline in 2026 due to increased production, but specific to EU routes, Ship & Bunker expects compliance costs to significantly offset the benefits from price decline. Even so, some additional drybulk capacity through 2026 would have been great for the bottom line and cashflows, and I say this particularly because containership charters already give us some visibility into future revenues. The contracted backlog for containership charters has now grown to $4.1 billion, with about 18% or $745 million of that added just in Q3 alone. So, the problem here is not really a problem, but one of missed opportunity.

That doesn't really affect my Buy rating and bull view, because now that the balance sheet is much stronger and shareholder equity is still growing due to higher retained earnings, mainly, the valuation disconnect crops up again. The stock has gained 33% since my last article but the P/B remains at a depressed 0.46, forward. That's still a good deal, in my opinion.

What cinches that deal is the TTM yield of 3.6% and forward yield of 3.8%. That's not much on the surface, but the kind of buyback activity we've seen this past year adds up to the shareholder side. I referred to a slowdown in buybacks at the top of this article, and you can see that here.

So, on a total return basis, DAC is still attractive. The dividend is steady and growing, although not aggressively so. Cashflow preservation is paramount, so I can see why management is being a little careful with its drybulk capacity increase. The company has ample dry powder to execute a capex burst if suitable vessels come up for sale. Not likely, with supply already so stretched, but it's still an optionality.

Buybacks will likely continue at a more muted level at these prices, but since the valuation is still attractive, it can offer reasonable buyback yields in the 5% to 6% range. That and the strong backlog profile might actually help DAC breach $100 over the next few quarters. Hopefully, it'll be more long-lived this time.

On the whole, I'll reiterate my Buy rating now and revisit this in the new year.

data.title: Broadcom: AI Silicon And High-Margin Software Create A Profitability Machine
data.description:
Thesis

I find Broadcom (AVGO) to be in a good position for strong future returns because it has experienced substantial growth and a transformative shift to its core businesses. Furthermore, it is extremely profitable and has a healthy financial foundation. The company is a major semiconductor producer and stands to directly benefit from the continued development of AI, while leveraging revenue from its infrastructure software portfolio.

Broadcom Overview

Broadcom specializes in pursuing a dual strategy targeting custom hardware and high-margin infrastructure software. This positioning places it at the center of the current AI boom. I believe that this unique mix makes Broadcom an industry leader with strong profitability metrics.

The company’s growth largely resides in the semiconductor segment, which is intensely focused on infrastructure for next-generation artificial intelligence. In particular, Broadcom is a key designer of custom processors and manufactures advanced networking solutions. These networking solutions are pivotal for the scaling of massive data centers, which further entrenches the company at the center of the AI revolution.

Broadcom’s Catalysts

Strategic Customer Wins

I see one of the most important catalysts for Broadcom is securing major contracts for deploying 10 gigawatts of custom AI accelerators and Ethernet networking systems. Additionally, it disclosed a $10 billion order for AI ASICs. These deals have the potential to be transformative for the company and can help further turbocharge its already healthy financial status. Some estimates suggest that these deals could add as much as $100 billion to the company and $8.00 in EPS.

Networking Leadership and Innovation

A major catalyst for Broadcom is the unveiling of Thor Ultra, which is the company’s new 800G AI Ethernet Network Interface Chip. This chip is highly innovative in that it will be able to connect XPUs more efficiently and drive far more parameters during AI workloads. As a result, it is possible to utilize more chips to train and run large language models.

Financial Overview: The Foundation of Growth and Profitability

An analysis of Broadcom's top-line performance shows a powerful and expanding enterprise. The company's Trailing Twelve Months revenue stands at an impressive $59.93 billion, which reflects a strong year-over-year growth rate of 28.01%. When I look at the company’s longer-term revenue growth, it remains exceptional. Broadcom is posting a 3-Year Compound Annual Growth Rate of 23.67% and a 5-Year CAGR of 20.90%. This is a testament to the health of the company’s core business model.

The quality of this revenue is driven by a two-pronged strategy. The traditional semiconductor solution segment remains the primary engine, which is fueled by high-growth areas like AI custom silicon, advanced networking, and high-speed connectivity. Critically, the software segment has been fundamentally transformed by the acquisition of VMware. This significantly increases the software segment’s contribution and transitions the overall model toward a sticky subscription-based revenue stream with high margins. This strategic pivot is driving expansion and predictable income for the company. Despite its impressive growth, the trajectory is expected to continue as consensus estimates for next year’s revenue are $63.34 billion.

Elite Profitability and Margins

Broadcom’s superior structural positioning is most evident when examining its profitability metrics. It consistently earns an A+ Profitability Grade. The TTM Gross Margin is a remarkable 77.19%, which far exceeds the overall Sector Median of 49.49%. I see this massive percentage difference as clearly suggesting exceptional intellectual property, immense scale, and superior pricing power in its core markets.

The operational efficiency continues down the income statement, with the TTM EBITDA Margin at 54.64% and the Net Income Margin at a stellar 31.59%. These figures cement Broadcom's status as a best-in-class operator, delivering profit margins that are significantly higher than the sector median. When assessing earnings quality, I see it as important to compare the Normalized Non-GAAP EPS against the GAAP EPS. This difference often highlights substantial non-recurring acquisition-related costs, such as the amortization of intangible assets and stock-based compensation resulting from its major deals.

Cash Flow and Capital Allocation

I believe that a key strength of the company is its robust Free Cash Flow generation. The consistency of this performance is backed by strong Cash Flow from Operating Activities, confirming its ability to turn revenue into usable cash.

Broadcom utilizes this massive cash generation capacity to fuel a strong capital return strategy. The company is committed to dividend growth, boasting a Forward Growth rate of 12.02%. I believe this dividend is highly sustainable because it is supported by FCF generation that easily covers shareholder distributions. This is indicated by a manageable TTM Payout Ratio of 57.20%, supported by the company’s Free Cash Flow generation.

Balance Sheet and Debt Profile

As a company built heavily on strategic acquisitions, I must acknowledge the Acquisition-Driven Leverage on the balance sheet. Broadcom carries a significant amount of debt, with TTM Total Debt at $64.23 billion and Net Debt at $53.51 billion. However, the company's formidable cash generation capability provides comfort regarding its debt service. Comparing the Net Debt of $53.51 to its TTM EBITDA of $32.726 billion allows me to calculate a ratio of approximately 1.63x. This ratio shows that the company is capable of covering its net debt with cash from under two years of operations. This is a manageable leverage level for a company this profitable.

Setting a Price Target for Broadcom

When I look at Broadcom's current valuation, the first thing that jumps out is the high premium it trades at. The company’s Forward Non-GAAP P/E is 50.77, and its TTM EV/EBITDA is 51.05. Unsurprisingly, this places its valuation grade at a stark “F”. To put the premium into perspective, the Forward P/E is 121.79% higher than the Information Technology Sector Median.

Peer Comparison

I compared Broadcom’s multiples against key competitors like TSM, AMD, MU, QCOM, and INTC. While many peers trade at lower multiples, I believe Broadcom deserves this premium due to two factors highlighted in the previous section, which are its best-in-class A+ Profitability Grade and its exceptional Revenue and EPS Growth trajectory. The market is effectively pricing in the certainty of high-margin, recurring revenue from the Software Segment and the explosive growth in its AI-driven Semiconductor solutions.

PEG Ratio Analysis

I need to calculate the Price-to-Earnings Growth Ratio to determine whether the premium is justified by growth. This ratio compares the forward multiple against future earnings growth. Typically, a value near 1.0 is considered fairly valued.

Using the Forward Non-GAAP P/E of 50.77 with the Fiscal Year 2025 EPS Growth rate of 38.58% is an effective option. Dividing 50.77 by 38.58 results in 1.31, suggesting it is only at a slight premium. Additionally, it suggests it is not overvalued compared to many other tech companies. This metric confirms to me that the valuation, while high on an absolute P/E basis, is defensible given the strength of the company’s future prospects.

Focus on Future Earnings

The primary driver of my price target is the consensus long-term Non-GAAP EPS estimate. I will focus on the Fiscal Year 2027 estimate, which stands at $12.24. I feel this estimate represents a compelling base for determining the company's intrinsic value several years out.

Given Broadcom’s dominant market position, high margins, and persistent double-digit growth, I believe it warrants a forward multiple higher than the blended sector average. The consensus estimate for Broadcom’s P/E in 2027 is 27.98. To account for its superior quality and justify a strong rating, I am comfortable applying a P/E multiple of 40x to the 2027 estimated earnings. This target multiple represents a discount to the current 50.77x P/E, implying a slight normalization, but it remains a premium to the estimated 2027 P/E, reflecting my conviction in the company’s execution.

Calculating the Target Price

Using the 2027 consensus EPS and my selected target multiple, the Target Price is calculated by multiplying the target EPS of $12.24 by the multiple of 40, which yields $489.60.

Based on this comparable multiples valuation using future earnings, I establish a Price Target of $490 for Broadcom. This target represents a significant increase from the current share price of approximately $346. I assign a Buy rating, which is predicated on the company's financial excellence and the compelling potential for future earnings growth.

Risks To Broadcom

Valuation Premium And Competition

Broadcom currently trades at a high premium, and I have some unease as to whether it will be able to effectively compete against Nvidia (NVDA). So far, the company has been successful in this regard, but the competition in this sector is fierce. Mistakes by Broadcom or breakthroughs from rivals pose a problem for the company’s continued growth.

Geopolitical Turbulence

Global supply chains are crucial for the development of AI chips, and this exposes Broadcom to geopolitical risks. This is especially notable following China's Ban on AI chip purchases from U.S. firms. Furthermore, key producers in Taiwan further expose the company to potential disruptions.

Conclusion

Broadcom is strongly positioned for continued growth thanks to its powerful dual strategy that combines custom AI semiconductor solutions with the stable subscription-based revenue from its acquired infrastructure software portfolio. While the company trades at a notable valuation premium, its elite profitability and exceptional long-term earnings trajectory justify the current price. As a result, my belief remains high, and leads me to assign a Buy rating with a price target of $490.
